content.problemStatement:
Chef was impressed by an array AAA of NNN non-negative integers. But, he was asked to solve the following problem in order to get this array.

Given a non-negative integer kkk, find the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) (1≤i<j≤N)(1 \leq i < j \leq N)(1≤i<j≤N) such that the following condition holds true:

(Ai(A_i(Ai​ ∣|∣ Aj)A_j)Aj​) +++ (Ai⊕Aj)(A_i \oplus A_j)(Ai​⊕Aj​) +++ (Ai(A_i(Ai​ &\&& Aj)A_j)Aj​) =k= k=k +Aj+ A_j+Aj​, where


(Ai∣Aj)(A_i | A_j)(Ai​∣Aj​) denotes Bitwise OR operation,
(Ai(A_i(Ai​ ⊕\oplus⊕ Aj)A_j)Aj​) denotes Bitwise XOR operation,
(Ai(A_i(Ai​ &\&& Aj)A_j)Aj​) denotes Bitwise AND operation.
You, being Chef's friend, help him get this array by solving the above problem.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and kkk, the number of integers in the array AAA and the non-negative integer kkk respectively.
The next line contains NNN space-separated non-negative integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, the elements of the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing the number of pairs satisfying the mentioned condition.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two space‑separated integers N and k. The next line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of pairs (i, j) with 1 ≤ i < j ≤ N that satisfy (Ai | Aj) + (Ai xor Aj) + (Ai & Aj) = k + Aj.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: only one element, no pair exists.\nTest case 2: pairs (1,2) and (2,3) satisfy Ai + (Ai xor Aj) = k (8). For (1,2): 1 + (1 xor 6) = 1 + 7 = 8. For (2,3): 6 + (6 xor 4) = 6 + 2 = 8.

solution.approach: Observe that (Ai | Aj) + (Ai & Aj) = Ai + Aj (bitwise identity). Hence the condition becomes Ai + Aj + (Ai xor Aj) = k + Aj, which simplifies to Ai + (Ai xor Aj) = k. Therefore for a fixed i we need Aj such that Ai xor Aj = k - Ai. Let target = k - Ai (if target < 0 there is no solution). The required value of Aj is val = Ai xor target. While scanning the array from right to left we keep a hash‑map that stores frequencies of values that appear to the right of the current index. For each i we add freq[val] to the answer and then increment freq[Ai]. This counts exactly the pairs with i < j. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case with O(N) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the hash map storing frequencies of suffix elements.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it)); k = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        freq = {}
        ans = 0
        for Ai in reversed(a):
            if k >= Ai:
                target = k - Ai
                need = Ai ^ target
                ans += freq.get(need, 0)
            freq[Ai] = freq.get(Ai, 0) + 1
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
